metadata.title: Solar PV Fault & Degradation Knowledge Base — Multi-Manufacturer
metadata.scope: Utility-scale and commercial PV inverters and modules
metadata.note: Fault codes are indicative — always cross-reference with manufacturer service manual for specific firmware version
metadata.sources_note: Sources added from v1.2 — includes field experience from 5 French utility-scale PV sites (8.2 France internal reports 2021-2024), Sungrow EMEA Academy fault code documentation, First Solar technical papers, NREL yield modelling research, IEA PVPS monitoring standards, and industry sources.

inverter_manufacturers.fault_log_export: SolarInfo portal > Device > Event Log > export CSV

faults.name: Grid Overvoltage
symptoms: Short trips 10–30 min mid-day on high-irradiance days
Correlates with peak export
root_causes: MV transformer tap too high
Reactive power export stacking voltage
Long LV cable impedance
actions: Adjust transformer tap down 2.5–5%
Enable Q-droop (cos φ = 0.90 leading)
Coordinate with DSO on MV quality

faults.name: DC Insulation / Earth Fault
symptoms: Trip after rain or heavy dew
Alarm: 'ISO fault' or 'insulation resistance low'
Repeated trips on same inverter
root_causes: Damaged DC cable insulation
Water ingress in module junction boxes
Loose or incompatible MC4 connectors
Combiner box moisture
actions: Megger test all strings at 1000 V DC (target >1 MΩ)
Use Sungrow Riso per MPPT channel to isolate fault
Replace damaged cable, seal glands
Inspect and re-crimp all MC4 connectors

faults.name: AC Contactor Wear / Failure
symptoms: Fails to reconnect after trip
Audible clicking with no production
Alarm: 'relay fault'
root_causes: Contact pitting from frequent arcing
Contactor rated for fewer operations than actual trip count
actions: Extract trip event count from SolarInfo — if >500/year, replace contactor proactively
Use OEM spare rated for 1 million mechanical operations
Resolve root-cause of frequent tripping first

faults.name: Overtemperature / Fan Failure
symptoms: De-rating or trip 12:00–16:00 in summer
Cabinet hot to touch
Alarm: 'overtemperature' or 'fan fault'
root_causes: Ambient >45°C at cabinet (direct sun)
Seized fan bearings
Blocked air filters
actions: Replace seized fans with OEM brushless DC equivalent
Clean/replace air filters every 6 months
Install sunshade if ambient regularly >45°C

faults.name: MPPT Startup Failure (Low Irradiance)
symptoms: Late morning start vs neighbouring inverters
Fails to restart after passing cloud in winter
root_causes: MPPT minimum threshold too high
String Vmpp below minimum MPPT range (200 V)
Partial shading reducing string voltage
actions: Lower startup power threshold from 1.0% to 0.5% of rated power
Clear new shading sources from affected strings
IV-curve at low irradiance to check mismatch

faults.name: DC Overvoltage (Cold Morning)
symptoms: Refuses to start on cold mornings
Trip immediately at sunrise after cold night
root_causes: String Voc at low temperature exceeds inverter 1500 V DC max
Mis-designed string — too many modules in series
actions: Calculate cold-weather Voc for site minimum temperature
Verify string count in as-built drawings
Reduce modules per string by 1 if structurally over-voltage

faults.name: AC Relay Fault
symptoms: Inverter fails to reconnect after trip
Audible relay click absent at restart
Repeated fault on same inverter despite no external cause
root_causes: Contact pitting from frequent arcing (>500 trips/year)
Failed SPD (surge protection device) with short to earth in LV cabinet
Low-impedance neutral-to-earth fault in MV transformer or LV network
Internal relay contacts worn beyond rated mechanical operations
actions: Listen for relay click during restart — absent click = failed relay
Measure phase-to-earth voltage at AC output terminals: if <100V indicates external neutral-earth fault
Inspect and test all SPDs in LV cabinet for earth short
Extract trip count from iSolarCloud — if >500/year, replace relay proactively
Resolve root cause of frequent tripping before relay replacement

faults.name: IGBT / Ambient Overtemperature
symptoms: Single IGBT module temperature diverges from others
Inverter shuts down 12:00–16:00 in summer
Cabinet hot to touch; fan noise absent or abnormal
root_causes: Seized or failed cooling fan bearings
Blocked air inlet filters (cotton or agricultural dust)
Insufficient ventilation clearance (<500 mm around enclosure)
South-facing row of inverters without shade canopy in direct sun
actions: Verify all fans spinning on startup; replace any that are stuck
Clean/replace air inlet filters every 6 months minimum
Ensure 500 mm minimum clearance around inverter enclosure
Install shade canopy if ambient regularly >45°C
For Code 036: if one IGBT temperature diverges from others, replace IGBT module

faults.name: DC Insulation Fault — String-Level Diagnostic
symptoms: Fault triggered after rain or heavy dew
Repeated fault on same inverter
Alarm: 'insulation resistance low' or ISO fault
root_causes: Damaged MC4 connector — third-party connectors incompatible with Sungrow tolerances
Water ingress into module junction boxes
Cable jacket damaged by animal chewing or tracker drive rail pinching
PV array Riso below 50 kΩ (default threshold)
actions: Systematic string isolation: disconnect all strings, reconnect one by one — string that re-triggers fault contains the defect
Megger test all strings at 1000 V DC (target >1 MΩ)
Inspect and re-crimp all MC4 connectors; replace third-party connectors with OEM Sungrow-compatible type
Inspect cable routes under tracker rails for pinching
Seal all junction box cable glands

faults.name: MPPT String Mismatch / MPPT Overload from Wiring Error
symptoms: Single inverter PR permanently 10–15% below fleet with no seasonal variation
No fault alarms raised
Clipping visible on affected MPPT channels at lower irradiance than normal
root_causes: Unequal number of modules per string on same MPPT input — voltage mismatch causes one string to pull the other off MPP
Overloaded MPPT input (more strings than rated) — clips at rated input power causing permanent energy loss
DC cabling design error assigning wrong string count to certain MPPT inputs
actions: Verify string count and module count per MPPT from single-line diagram vs. physical site
Calculate total DC power per MPPT vs. rated input power — overloaded if >100% of rated MPPT input
Check maximum string Voc at minimum site temperature against inverter max DC voltage limit
Run iSolarCloud I-V curve scan to identify anomalous MPPT channels
Rebalance string connections to equalise MPPT loading
sources: Previous report: Mas d'En Ramis (PTR3 Inv13) — 8.2 France internal analysis 2024

faults.name: Inverter Clipping Loss Underestimation in Hourly SCADA
symptoms: Yield model predicts higher energy than SCADA shows during high-irradiance periods
PR appears lower than expected in summer midday hours
root_causes: SCADA logs hourly averages; clipping is a sub-hourly event that is averaged away
Clipping occurs 3.5–4.0% of annual operating hours at ILR 1.34
Hourly TMY data introduces positive yield bias vs. actual 5-min reality
actions: Configure SCADA to log at 5-minute resolution for all power and irradiance channels
Use sub-hourly irradiance data for yield model validation
Apply clipping correction factor when comparing hourly SCADA PR to hourly yield model

faults.name: AFCI Arc Fault Warning
symptoms: AFCI warning alarm in iSolarCloud
Intermittent DC-side fault
No obvious cable damage visible on inspection
root_causes: Series arc in DC string cable — damaged insulation, loose terminal connection
Parallel arc at module junction box terminal
Induced false positive from specific cable routing geometries
actions: Inspect DC cable routing and insulation integrity thoroughly on affected string
Thermographic inspection of all DC junction boxes on affected MPPT
Re-enable AFCI function after verification and resolution
If recurring false positives: review cable routing and separation from grounded structures

faults.name: French Grid Curtailment / Q-Regulation Active Power Loss
symptoms: Unexplained PR dip during high-irradiance periods with no fault alarms
Active power limited below inverter rated output
Curtailment commands visible in RTE/Enedis SCADA logs
root_causes: RTE or Enedis curtailment dispatch during negative-price or grid congestion periods (~3 TWh curtailed in France in 2025)
Q-regulation (reactive power) commanded by DSO consuming a share of inverter apparent power capacity — reduces available P at sites near AC connection limit
Curtailment events not correctly flagged in plant SCADA, appearing as unexplained underperformance
actions: Verify RTE/Enedis curtailment command timestamps are logged in plant SCADA and correctly labelled
Exclude curtailment periods from contractual PR and availability calculations
Verify Q setpoint compliance — Sungrow SG250HX supports night reactive power delivery (Q at P=0) which satisfies certain French grid code obligations
Coordinate with DSO to receive curtailment log for reconciliation against SCADA

inverter_manufacturers.monitoring_platform: SMA Sunny Portal / Sunny Explorer / SMA Energy System Home
inverter_manufacturers.fault_log_export: Sunny Portal > Plant > Device Configuration > Event List > Export

faults.name: DC Ground / Earth Fault
symptoms: Trip with 'insulation fault' alarm
SMA ISOLAN monitoring triggers at <600 kΩ (much more sensitive than most brands)
Intermittent fault correlated with rain
root_causes: Damaged DC wiring insulation
Module frame-to-cell insulation degradation
Moisture in junction box or combiner
actions: SMA ISOLAN stores faulty MPPT channel — use Sunny Explorer to identify
Megger test at 1000 V DC on isolated strings
Replace damaged cables; seal all cable entry glands
faults.note: SMA's ISOLAN insulation monitoring is more sensitive than Sungrow/Huawei (triggers at 600 kΩ vs 30 kΩ) — some false positives in wet conditions are expected

faults.name: Arc Fault Detection (AFCI) — False Positive
symptoms: Trip with 'arc fault detected' alarm
Clears on manual reset with no physical fault found
More common in long cable runs with high capacitance
root_causes: Long DC cables causing capacitive discharge waveforms that mimic arc signature
Slightly loose MC4 connector (even without actual arcing)
AFCI algorithm false trigger in high-humidity conditions
actions: Inspect all MC4 connectors on affected MPPT channel — re-crimp any loose ones
Check cable routing for any abrasion points
If structural false positives: consult SMA on AFCI sensitivity threshold adjustment (service password required)
faults.note: AFCI is a safety feature required in some markets — do NOT disable without OEM guidance

faults.name: Grid Parameter Exceedance (Overvoltage / Overfrequency)
symptoms: Trip with 'grid overvoltage' or 'grid overfrequency'
SMA has tighter default trip thresholds than many brands
Alarm: 'AC voltage too high'
root_causes: MV transformer tap set too high for full export conditions
SMA default voltage trip set at 253 V (110% of 230 V) — some installations need adjustment
Reactive power export not configured
actions: Review and adjust trip thresholds within grid code allowances (EN 50549-1 in France: 264 V max)
Enable reactive power provision: cos φ characteristic or Q(U) droop
Engage with DSO if grid voltage structurally high

faults.name: Fan / Cooling Failure
symptoms: Alarm: 'cooling fan fault'
Internal temperature rising trend in Sunny Portal
De-rating in summer before trip
root_causes: Fan bearing wear — SMA Tripower fans typically last 5–8 years
Dust accumulation on fan blades reducing airflow
Internal heatsink fins blocked by debris
actions: Replace fans proactively at 5-year inspection — SMA fan replacement kit available
Clean heatsink fins with compressed air
Monitor internal temperature trend in Sunny Portal

faults.name: Speedwire / Webconnect Communication Loss
symptoms: Inverter appears offline in Sunny Portal despite running
SCADA shows data gaps for SMA devices only
Alarm: 'no SMA Speedwire connection'
root_causes: Ethernet cable fault or switch port failure
IP address conflict on local network
Router firmware update disrupting DHCP assignment
Sunny Home Manager or Cluster Controller firmware incompatibility
actions: Power-cycle the Sunny Home Manager / Cluster Controller
Check Ethernet physical layer — replace cable if >10 m without quality shielding
Assign static IP to inverter in router DHCP table
Update Speedwire firmware via Sunny Explorer

inverter_manufacturers.fault_log_export: FusionSolar SmartPVMS > O&M > Alarm Management > Export

faults.name: DC Insulation Fault
symptoms: Trip with alarm 'PV1/PV2 insulation resistance too low'
Often recovers after dry weather
Alarm log timestamp matches rainfall events
root_causes: Water ingress in module junction boxes or string combiner
Damaged DC cable insulation
MC4 connector not fully engaged
actions: FusionSolar identifies fault per MPPT — export alarm log and cross-reference to string plan
Megger test at 1000 V DC on isolated strings
Inspect MC4 connectors; replace non-OEM types with certified equivalents

faults.name: Arc Fault Detection (AFCI)
symptoms: Trip with 'arc fault' alarm
May recur on same MPPT input
Often no visual evidence of burning on initial inspection
root_causes: Loose MC4 connector creating intermittent arcing
Abraded cable insulation with intermittent contact to metalwork
Huawei's AFCI algorithm is among the most sensitive in the industry
actions: Inspect all connectors on reported MPPT string — torque-pull test each MC4
Check cable routing for contact with sharp metalwork
Replace any connector with carbon tracking (black marks)
faults.note: Huawei AFCI must not be disabled. Any MC4 arcing is a fire risk — treat as priority regardless of energy impact

faults.name: SmartLogger / Communications Gateway Failure
symptoms: All Huawei inverters appear offline in FusionSolar simultaneously
SCADA shows complete data gap across all devices
Inverters running normally locally (SUN2000 display shows OK)
root_causes: SmartLogger3000 hardware failure
SmartLogger firmware crash (restart usually recovers)
4G SIM card failure or data plan expired
Ethernet or RS485 cable break between SmartLogger and inverters
actions: Power-cycle SmartLogger (hold reset button 10 s)
Check 4G signal strength LED — replace SIM if no signal
Verify RS485 termination resistance (120 Ω at each end)
If repeated crashes: update SmartLogger firmware via FusionSolar

faults.name: Overtemperature / Thermal Throttling
symptoms: Power ramp-down 12:00–16:00 in summer
FusionSolar shows 'derating' status
Cabinet exceeds 75°C internal temperature
root_causes: Ambient temperature >45°C at cabinet
Cabinet installed in direct sun without shade
Air filter blocked
Heat sink fins fouled with dust — common in 5+ year old units
actions: Clean heat sink fins with compressed air annually
Replace air filters every 6 months in dusty environments
Install reflective shade canopy over inverter if ambient routinely >45°C

faults.name: Grid Voltage / Frequency Out of Range
symptoms: Trip correlated with peak export periods
FusionSolar alarm: 'AC overvoltage' or 'grid frequency too high'
root_causes: LV/MV transformer tap settings incorrect for export conditions
Grid code reactive power settings not configured
actions: Enable reactive power: configure cos φ characteristic or Q(V) droop in FusionSolar
Coordinate with DSO on transformer tap adjustment
Review French RPfR grid code compliance (cos φ = 0.85 inductive to 0.95 capacitive)

faults.name: Optimizer Offline / Mismatch (if SmartPV optimizers installed)
symptoms: FusionSolar optimizer view shows red modules
Specific yield heatmap shows individual module-level underperformance
Optimizer offline alarm in FusionSolar
root_causes: Power line communication (PLC) signal loss between optimizer and SmartLogger
Optimizer firmware inconsistency after partial update
Physical optimizer failure — internal MOSFET failure
actions: Trigger remote optimizer firmware update from FusionSolar
Check PLC signal path — long cable runs or filters can attenuate signal
Replace physically failed optimizers — swap and re-commission in FusionSolar

inverter_manufacturers.fault_log_export: Aurora Vision > Plant > Events > Export CSV

faults.name: DC Ground Fault
symptoms: Alarm: 'ground fault detected'
Inverter shuts down and latches — requires manual reset
root_causes: Cable insulation failure
Junction box moisture ingress
actions: ABB Aurora Vision identifies fault to MPPT level
Megger test at 1000 V DC per string
Replace faulty cable; seal junction boxes

faults.name: Grid Overvoltage / Frequency Fault
symptoms: Short trips at peak production
Alarm: 'grid voltage outside limits'
root_causes: Transformer tap too high
No reactive power configured
actions: Enable reactive power provision in ABB settings
Adjust transformer tap
Verify trip thresholds match EN 50549-1

faults.name: Fan Failure / Overtemperature
symptoms: De-rating in summer afternoons
Alarm: 'overtemperature' or 'fan fault'
root_causes: Fan bearing failure
Heatsink fouled with dust
actions: Replace fans proactively at 5-year service
Clean heatsink fins with compressed air at biannual O&M

faults.name: DC Isolation Fault
symptoms: Alarm: 'isolation fault'
Trip after rain
root_causes: Cable insulation damage
Junction box moisture ingress
actions: Fronius identifies faulty PV input — log shows MPPT channel
Megger test at 1000 V DC
Replace damaged cable or junction box seal

faults.name: Grid Voltage / Frequency Out of Range
symptoms: Short trips at peak export
Alarm: 'AC voltage too high'
More common at end-of-feeder installations
root_causes: Grid voltage high due to export stacking
Fronius default trip thresholds are strict
actions: Enable Fronius Q-mode reactive power control
Adjust voltage trip threshold via Fronius interface (within EN 50549-1 limits)
Engage DSO if structurally high voltage

faults.name: Fan / Cooling Failure
symptoms: De-rating in summer
Alarm: 'temperature too high'
root_causes: Fan bearing failure
Blocked ventilation
actions: Replace fan at 5-year service interval
Check rear clearance (minimum 200 mm from wall)

faults.name: DC Ground Fault
symptoms: Alarm: 'ground fault' or 'Riso below minimum'
Trip after rain
root_causes: Cable or junction box moisture ingress
MC4 connector incompatibility
actions: Ingeteam INGECON identifies fault per string input
Megger test; replace damaged cables
Inspect MC4 connectors

faults.name: Grid Overvoltage
symptoms: Short trip at peak export
Correlated with high production periods
root_causes: MV transformer tap too high
No reactive power provision
actions: Enable reactive power in Ingeteam settings
Adjust transformer tap
Verify compliance with RD 1699 / EN 50549-1

issues.name: Soiling & Surface Contamination
symptoms: PR decline Apr–Sep
Recovery after autumn rain
root_causes: Agricultural dust, road dust, bird droppings on flat glass surface
actions: Structured cleaning: May and August
Bird droppings: remove within 48 h
Pre/post-rain specific yield comparison to quantify soiling rate

issues.name: DC Insulation Degradation (Cell-to-Frame)
symptoms: Inverter DC insulation fault traced to specific string
Fault correlates with wet weather
root_causes: Thin-film cell-to-glass-frame insulation ageing
Micro-cracks from hail or mechanical stress allowing moisture
actions: Megger per string at 1000 V DC
EL imaging to locate micro-cracked cells
Replace modules with <100 kΩ insulation resistance
issues.energy_impact: HIGH locally — causes inverter trip; full string offline

issues.name: Junction Box Failure / Bypass Diode Overload
symptoms: Thermal hotspot on junction box in IR imaging
String open-circuit
DC insulation alarm on inverter
root_causes: Persistent bird dropping shadow activating bypass diode repeatedly
Sealant degradation allowing moisture
actions: Replace all 3 bypass diodes when one fails
Remove bird droppings within 48 h
Annual thermal imaging campaign
issues.energy_impact: 33% loss per module when one bypass diode group is shorted

issues.name: Light Soaking (Expected — Positive Effect)
symptoms: Year-1 PR slightly above nameplate predictions
root_causes: CdTe photodoping increases carrier lifetime in first 100–200 kWh/m² cumulative irradiance
actions: No action required — exclude first 3 months from long-term PR benchmarking
issues.energy_impact: Positive — 1–3% above nameplate in first months

issues.name: Long-Term Degradation
symptoms: PR decline 0.3–0.5%/year when corrected for soiling and irradiance
root_causes: CdS/CdTe junction interface changes — slower than c-Si
actions: Annual IV-curve sampling (5% of modules) to track rate
If >0.5%/year: submit First Solar warranty claim
issues.warranty_reference: First Solar: 97.5% at year 1, declining to 86.5% at year 25 (0.5%/year linear guarantee)

issues.name: Micro-Cracking (Hail or Mechanical)
symptoms: Dark patches in EL imaging
Localised power loss 2–5% per affected module
root_causes: Hail >25 mm diameter on glass-glass construction
Point loads from improper mounting
actions: EL imaging post-hail event
Replace modules with cracks >50% of cell area
File insurance claim if hail confirmed
issues.energy_impact: 2–5% per module — significant if multiple modules affected

issues.name: Potential-Induced Degradation (PID) — TCO Corrosion
issues.frequency: Uncommon — risk elevated at negative string end in ungrounded systems
symptoms: Power loss concentrated at modules at highest negative potential relative to ground
EL imaging shows TCO corrosion around module clamps and edge regions
Voc and fill factor reduction without significant Isc loss
Loss gradient along string — negative-end modules worst
root_causes: Sodium migration from soda-lime glass superstrate toward CdTe junction under high system voltage + elevated temperature
TCO layer attacked by atomic hydrogen produced by reaction between moisture and reduced sodium at edge seal
Moisture ingress through inadequate edge sealing — soda-lime glass has known moisture diffusion path at periphery
actions: EL (electroluminescence) imaging survey on a sample of modules — focus on negative-string-end modules first
IV-curve testing to characterise Voc and fill factor loss signature
Check edge seal integrity on suspect modules
Verify system voltage configuration — grounding one string end reduces PID driving voltage
Note: Series 6 CuRe has improved edge sealing and redesigned back contact that significantly mitigates this mechanism
issues.degradation_rate_note: Field data from a 2.3 MW CdTe plant after 6 years showed 43% power loss at negative string end vs 17% at positive end

issues.name: CuRe vs. Legacy Cu Back Contact — Degradation Rate Context
symptoms: N/A — use for benchmarking annual degradation rate
root_causes: Original Series 6 (Cu back contact): copper migration along CdTe grain boundaries causes recombination centres, Voc and fill factor loss at rate ~0.5%/yr with ~2% initial stabilisation loss
Series 6 CuRe (Group V dopant): copper replaced, eliminating migration pathway and initial stabilisation dip — stable from day one
actions: Determine which back-contact technology the site uses before applying warranted degradation benchmark
For pre-CuRe modules: expect first 30–90 days of production below P50 (initial stabilisation); compare against warranted stabilisation curve
For CuRe modules: output should be stable from commissioning; any early PR decline requires investigation

degradation_benchmarks.excess_decline_threshold: PR decline >1%/yr requires priority investigation: module EL/IV testing, soiling, inverter efficiency trending, irradiance sensor drift check

issues.name: Temperature Coefficient Advantage — CdTe vs c-Si
symptoms: CdTe plant PR appears to outperform c-Si benchmark in summer — this is expected, not a fault
root_causes: CdTe Pmax temperature coefficient: −0.28%/°C vs c-Si −0.35 to −0.50%/°C — CdTe loses less power per degree of cell temperature rise
actions: Do not apply c-Si PR benchmarks directly to CdTe plants — the expected PR is higher in hot conditions
Summer PR declining toward c-Si levels may signal module degradation or soiling eroding the thermal advantage — investigate
Annual energy advantage of CdTe over equivalent c-Si: +4 to +9% in hot climates

issues.name: Hot Spot in Thin-Film CdTe
symptoms: Elevated cell temperature visible on IR thermography
Temperature gradient smaller than in c-Si (glass-glass encapsulation reduces surface delta-T)
Power loss without Isc degradation
root_causes: Local defect in TCO or back contact causing current to flow across resistive zone
Monolithic cell structure — hot spot can propagate to adjacent cells within module more readily than in c-Si
actions: Use high-sensitivity IR camera (NETD <50 mK) due to smaller surface temperature gradients vs c-Si
Perform thermographic survey under standard field conditions (>600 W/m² irradiance)
Identify affected modules for IV-curve testing to quantify fill factor loss
Note: CdTe has better partial shading tolerance than c-Si (linear shading response), but sustained reverse bias can cause stress in thin-film cell structure

module_technologies.lid_effect: Negative — LID 1–3% in first 50 kWh/m² (PERC also has LETID)

issues.name: LID — Light Induced Degradation
symptoms: PR 1–3% below nameplate in first 4–8 weeks of operation
Stabilises after ~50 kWh/m² cumulative irradiance
root_causes: Boron-oxygen defect formation in p-type silicon under illumination
actions: Expected behaviour — no field action required
Account for LID in first-year energy yield predictions (adjust by 1–2%)
issues.note: TOPCon and HJT modules have negligible LID — much less than standard PERC

issues.name: LETID — Light and Elevated Temperature Induced Degradation
symptoms: PR declines progressively in summer months over first 2–3 years beyond LID
Particularly pronounced in hot climates (>35°C operating cell temperature)
Partial recovery in winter
root_causes: Hydrogenated boron-oxygen defects activated at elevated cell temperatures (>50°C) under illumination
More severe in high-power PERC with dense cell packing
actions: Monitor year-on-year PR corrected for irradiance — decline >1%/year in years 1–3 may indicate LETID
Check if module manufacturer applies LeTID mitigation (light soaking at factory)
Raise potential warranty claim if degradation exceeds 2% in first year
issues.note: LETID partially recovers over time — full stabilisation typically by year 3–5
issues.energy_impact: 0.5–2% additional degradation beyond LID — largely recoverable by year 5

issues.name: PID — Potential Induced Degradation
issues.frequency: Common in positive-grounded or ungrounded systems with old PERC modules
symptoms: Uniform PR decline across all modules in a string
Decline worst in high-humidity summer months
Lower-voltage strings (near negative rail) least affected
root_causes: Negative potential on cell relative to frame drives sodium ions from glass into cell via EVA
Accelerated by high humidity, high temperature, and high string voltage
Risk varies by EVA formulation, glass composition, and cell design
actions: PID recovery: apply DC reverse bias at night using PID recovery box (1–2 weeks typical)
Long-term: replace EVA encapsulant with POE (polyolefin) which has much lower PID susceptibility
For new systems: specify POE encapsulant or TOPCon/HJT modules which are PID-immune
issues.energy_impact: Potentially severe: 10–30% loss in extreme cases; recovery typically 80–90% of lost output
issues.warranty_reference: Most manufacturers warrant PID loss <5% at 1000 V for 25 years — verify test certificate IEC 62804

issues.name: Micro-cracking (Cell Cracking)
symptoms: Dark branches or patches in EL imaging
Power loss 3–15% depending on crack pattern
Snail trails (discolouration along crack lines after years)
root_causes: Hail impact
Wind-induced flexing on modules with thin glass (<3.2 mm)
Improper clamping — over-tightened or misplaced clamps
Transport vibration
actions: EL imaging at commissioning (baseline) and biannual thereafter
Replace modules with 'inactive crack' patterns (EL shows cell area separated from bus bar)
Verify clamp positions match manufacturer drawing (mid-frame only for most bifacial)
issues.energy_impact: 3–15% per affected module — large-area inactive cracks are most damaging

issues.name: EVA Browning / Yellowing
symptoms: Yellow or brown discolouration visible through glass
Uniform power loss across whole module (all cells affected equally)
EL imaging normal — optical loss only
root_causes: UV degradation of EVA encapsulant accelerated by high temperature and UV exposure
Acetic acid release from EVA can cause corrosion of silver cell busbars
More common in older (<2018) modules using standard EVA without UV stabiliser
actions: No field repair possible — replace if power loss >5% attributable to browning
Modern POE and UV-resistant EVA formulations largely eliminate this failure mode
Document and submit warranty claim with Pmax measurements corrected for temperature
issues.energy_impact: 1–8% optical transmittance loss depending on severity

issues.name: Backsheet Delamination / Cracking
symptoms: White chalking or cracking visible on module rear
Bubbling or delamination at module edges or corners
Eventual moisture ingress causing corrosion of cell metallisation
root_causes: Non-fluorinated (PPE, PET) backsheet UV degradation after 5–10 years
Fluorinated backsheets (PVDF/KPK) much more resistant — now industry standard
Thermal cycling stress at edges
actions: Replace modules with active cracks in backsheet — moisture ingress risk causes accelerated further degradation
Apply moisture barrier tape as emergency measure pending replacement (6-month maximum)
For new procurement: require PVDF/PVDF or glass-glass construction
issues.energy_impact: Initially low — but if unchecked, moisture ingress causes rapid degradation

issues.name: Hotspot / Bypass Diode Failure
symptoms: Bright hotspot in thermal imaging — dramatically hotter than surrounding cells
Burn marks on rear of module or junction box
Module power loss 5–30%
root_causes: Persistent partial shading (bird droppings, leaves, lichen) causing reverse bias on shadowed cells
Bypass diode open-circuit failure causing entire bypass group to run in reverse bias
String mismatch with one module at lower irradiance
actions: Thermal imaging identifies affected modules — 200–600 W/m² irradiance best for hotspot detection
Remove shading source immediately
Replace bypass diode if open-circuit (all three diodes simultaneously)
Replace module if carbon track or burn marks visible on cells
issues.energy_impact: HIGH for affected module — if bypass diode fails open: one-third of module bypassed

issues.name: Snail Trails
symptoms: Dark brown or black lines following cell cracks, visible through glass after months/years
Slight power loss — typically <3% on its own
root_causes: Acetic acid from EVA reacts with silver cell busbars, depositing silver acetate along crack paths
Purely aesthetic in early stages — indicates underlying micro-cracks
actions: Snail trails themselves cause minimal power loss — focus on identifying underlying cracks via EL imaging
Replace module if EL shows large inactive crack area (even if no visible snail trail yet)
issues.energy_impact: Low on its own (<3%), but indicates micro-cracks which may worsen

issues.name: Back-Contact Delamination
issues.frequency: Rare — quality issue in earlier series
symptoms: Power loss without visible front-side damage
EL imaging shows dark patterns originating at cell edges
Blistering on module rear
root_causes: Adhesive failure in back-contact structure under thermal cycling
Moisture ingress at edge in humid climates
actions: EL imaging to identify extent
Replace affected modules
File warranty claim — Maxeon provides 40-year product warranty
issues.energy_impact: Potentially significant — back-contact failure removes entire cell groups

issues.name: Long-Term Degradation
symptoms: PR decline 0.2–0.3%/year — better than any other commercial technology
root_causes: n-type silicon with back-contact architecture has inherently superior stability
actions: Annual IV-curve sampling
Maxeon warranty: 92% at year 25 (0.25%/year) — file claim if exceeded

module_technologies.pid_risk: Low to Moderate (TOPCon lower than PERC)
module_technologies.lid_effect: Minimal for TOPCon / 1–2% for PERC bifacial

issues.name: Rear-Side Soiling
symptoms: Measured bifacial gain (5–15% expected) lower than modelled
Albedo benefit not appearing in yield
root_causes: Dust or concrete splatter on rear glass — often during installation
Ground-mounted bifacial with insufficient elevation (>300 mm recommended)
actions: Include rear glass cleaning in O&M programme — often forgotten
Measure rear irradiance with albedometer to confirm actual bifacial gain
Verify ground clearance and surface albedo matches model assumptions
issues.energy_impact: Loss of 3–8% of expected bifacial gain

issues.name: Improper Clamp Positioning (Bifacial-Specific)
symptoms: EL imaging shows dark stressed cells along clamp contact areas
Power loss localised to clamped rows
Micro-cracking progresses over first year
root_causes: Clamps placed on cell active area instead of on the frame-only zone
Many bifacial modules have specific clamp zones — check manufacturer drawing
Over-torqued clamps
actions: Verify clamp positions match manufacturer mounting specification
Use torque wrench at 20–25 Nm (manufacturer spec)
Relocate misplaced clamps during next site visit and monitor with EL imaging
issues.energy_impact: 2–10% per clamped module if active area cracked

diagnostic_matrix.description: Observed SCADA symptom → most probable fault categories to investigate first (any brand)

entries.tool_required: Inverter fault code export + Megger 1000 V DC
DSO MV relay event log + power quality recorder

entries.scada_symptom: Inverter PR low but availability normal (running but underproducing)
entries.tool_required: IV-curve tracer + EL imager + thermal camera

entries.scada_symptom: PR decline Apr–Sep, full recovery after autumn rain
entries.tool_required: Pre/post-rain specific yield comparison (no instruments needed)

entries.scada_symptom: DC insulation alarm on one inverter after rain
entries.tool_required: Megger 1000 V DC per string; identify faulty MPPT from inverter log

entries.scada_symptom: Production de-rates mid-day in summer only
investigate_first: Grid overvoltage at peak export (all brands)
entries.tool_required: Inverter internal temperature log + grid voltage log

entries.scada_symptom: Uniform PR decline across entire fleet (year-on-year, all inverters)
investigate_first: Module degradation — check technology (MOD-cSi-001 LID, MOD-cSi-002 LETID, MOD-CdTe-005)
entries.tool_required: IV-curve sampling + irradiance-corrected PR trend + pyranometer recalibration

entries.scada_symptom: AFCI trip (Huawei or SMA) — no visible damage found
investigate_first: MC4 connector not fully engaged (HW-002, SMA-002)
Long cable run capacitive false trigger
entries.tool_required: MC4 pull-force test; visual inspection for carbon tracking

entries.scada_symptom: All Huawei inverters offline in monitoring but producing locally
One inverter consistently 5–15% below fleet PR across both years
entries.tool_required: EL imaging + IV-curve + insulation resistance test

expected_seasonal_patterns.description: Normal seasonal behaviours that should NOT be flagged as underperformance — include these in report interpretations to avoid false alarms.

patterns.pattern: Lower specific yield in winter months
patterns.explanation: Winter months have shorter days and lower solar elevation angles, resulting in significantly less daily irradiation (GHI). Specific yield (kWh/kWp) will naturally be 30–60% lower than summer peak months depending on latitude. This is not underperformance.
patterns.correct_interpretation: Compare per-inverter DEVIATION from fleet mean within the same month — not absolute values across seasons. If all inverters track together in winter, the plant is performing correctly.
patterns.kpi_impact: Specific yield low; PR is largely weather-normalised and should be similar across seasons if plant is healthy.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Check PR rather than absolute production. If monthly PR is consistent year-round (±5%), the plant is fine. Only investigate if one inverter diverges from fleet mean within the same month.
patterns.flag_if: One or more inverters show significantly LOWER yield relative to fleet peers in the same winter month — this IS underperformance vs peers.
patterns.do_not_flag_if: All inverters show lower absolute yield in winter — this is expected.

patterns.pattern: Higher soiling losses in summer / dry season
patterns.explanation: Dust, pollen and bird droppings accumulate faster in dry summer months with less rainfall to wash panels. A 2–8% soiling loss is typical in dry climates. This causes a gradual drop in specific yield and PR through the dry season that partially recovers with first autumn rains.
patterns.correct_interpretation: Soiling is manageable, not a fault. It should appear as a gradual decline in fleet-mean specific yield from May onwards, with recovery after the first significant rain event.
patterns.kpi_impact: PR degrades 0.5–2% per month without cleaning. Specific yield dips below seasonal baseline.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Compare specific yield in the week immediately before and after a rain event (>5mm). A >3% yield step-change confirms active soiling. Plot rolling 7-day yield vs irradiation ratio.
applicable_technologies: All — CdTe more sensitive to dust on the TCO layer; bifacial loses bifacial gain preferentially on the rear
patterns.flag_if: Soiling exceeds 8% estimated loss per month, or if no cleaning programme exists at a site with measurable soiling rates.
patterns.do_not_flag_if: Gradual summer yield decline is consistent across all inverters and followed by post-rain recovery.

patterns.pattern: Higher PR in winter due to lower cell temperature
patterns.explanation: PV module efficiency increases at lower cell temperatures. Temperature coefficient of power is typically -0.35 to -0.45%/°C for mono-Si, and -0.26%/°C for CdTe. In winter, modules may run 20–30°C cooler than summer peak, yielding 7–15% higher electrical conversion efficiency, which shows as higher PR.
patterns.correct_interpretation: A higher PR in winter is expected and desirable — it does NOT offset the lower absolute production from reduced irradiation.
patterns.kpi_impact: Monthly PR may be 5–10 percentage points higher in winter vs summer for the same plant.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Plot PR vs ambient temperature to confirm negative correlation. Divergence from expected PR-temperature relationship may indicate a string fault masked by the temperature bonus.
patterns.flag_if: PR does NOT improve in winter compared to summer at a well-performing site — may indicate a persistent DC fault unrelated to temperature.
patterns.do_not_flag_if: PR is noticeably higher in winter — this is the expected thermal gain.

patterns.explanation: Inverters require minimum irradiance (~50 W/m²) before MPPT can lock onto the array voltage. At low sun angles, DC voltage may also be below the inverter's minimum start voltage. Production typically begins 20–40 minutes after sunrise and ends 20–40 minutes before sunset.
patterns.correct_interpretation: The morning/evening 'shoulders' of the daily production curve will be missing or truncated. This is normal and not a fault.
patterns.kpi_impact: Low impact in summer (short ramp-up vs long day). Greater proportional impact in winter when days are short.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Compare irradiance vs production scatter plot — if production begins consistently at the same irradiance threshold (~50–100 W/m²), the start delay is due to MPPT, not a fault.
applicable_technologies: All — threshold varies by inverter minimum start voltage and MPP range
patterns.flag_if: Production consistently starts more than 1 hour after the irradiance threshold is met, or stops well before the threshold drops below minimum.
patterns.do_not_flag_if: Production starts in the 30–45 minute window after irradiance rises above ~50 W/m².

patterns.explanation: A significant rainfall event (>5mm) typically washes accumulated soiling off module surfaces, resulting in a noticeable yield improvement in the 1–3 days following rain. This manifests as a sudden increase in specific yield and PR that then gradually declines again as soiling re-accumulates.
patterns.correct_interpretation: This is the clearest natural soiling measurement. The magnitude of the spike quantifies the pre-rain soiling loss.
patterns.kpi_impact: Yield step-change of 2–8% is typical after effective rain washoff.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Overlay rainfall data on daily specific yield chart. Consistent yield step-changes after rain events confirm soiling as the primary PR driver and quantify cleaning ROI.
patterns.flag_if: Post-rain yield improvement is absent at a site with known dust accumulation — may indicate stubborn contamination (bird droppings, adhesive dust) requiring mechanical cleaning.
patterns.do_not_flag_if: Production improves measurably in the days after rainfall — this confirms the soiling hypothesis.

patterns.pattern: Reduced production during and after extreme heat events
patterns.explanation: On very hot days (ambient >35°C), inverters may thermally derate output power to protect internal components (typically by 1–5% per degree above the rated ambient). Module power also drops due to temperature coefficient. Combined effect: plant output may be 10–20% below irradiation-predicted levels on the hottest summer days.
patterns.correct_interpretation: Short-duration thermal derating during heat waves is designed-in behaviour. It is not a fault unless derating is more severe than expected, more frequent than weather justifies, or persists after the heat event ends.
patterns.kpi_impact: Short-lived PR dip during peak summer heat. Not material to annual energy yield unless heat waves are prolonged.
patterns.diagnostic_test: Cross-reference low-PR days against ambient temperature records. If PR dip matches temperature extremes, it is thermal derating. If PR is low on normal-temperature days, investigate further.
applicable_technologies: All — inverter derating curves differ by manufacturer; Sungrow and Huawei central inverters typically derate above 45°C ambient
patterns.flag_if: Thermal derating is triggered at normal ambient temperatures (< 30°C), suggesting a cooling system fault (blocked air filters, failed fans).
patterns.do_not_flag_if: PR dips coincide with confirmed ambient temperature >35°C and recover immediately afterwards.

o_and_m_schedule.description: Recommended O&M inspection calendar — generic utility-scale PV

tasks.task: SCADA monitoring — inverter trips, data gaps, alarm review
Pyranometer dome cleaning; soiling visual check
Site walkthrough — vegetation, junction box visual, bird deterrent check
Thermal imaging (modules + junction boxes); inverter air filter clean; MC4 inspection
tasks.tools: IR camera, compressed air, torque wrench

tasks.task: Insulation resistance test all strings; IV-curve (5% sample); fan check; pyranometer calibration verify; AC/DC wiring inspection
EL imaging campaign; pyranometer factory calibration; contactor resistance check on high-trip units
100% IV-curve campaign; cable insulation survey; contactor replacement on inverters >300k operations

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Correlated Multi-Inverter Data Gaps = SCADA Fault
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: When multiple inverters across different PTRs share identical missing-data start/end timestamps, the cause is upstream (SCADA logger, communication link, network switch), not individual inverter hardware. Do not count as inverter downtime.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Compare missing-data timestamps across all inverters. Correlated gaps = systemic SCADA/communication failure.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Milhac d'Auberoche 2022, Mas d'En Ramis 2024

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Missing% + Null% ≈ 100% for Full Month = Probable Full Inverter Outage
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: If (missing_pct + null_pct) ≈ 100% for irradiance > 50 W/m² over a full calendar month on a single inverter, flag as probable full inverter outage. Requires on-site logbook cross-check to distinguish genuine shutdown from communication blackout.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Time-series inspection of missing + null rates per inverter per month. Combined 100% = inverter produced nothing all month.

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Null Power with Zero Missing Data = Inverter Online But Not Producing
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: Null power (power = 0 at irradiance > 50 W/m²) with zero missing data means the inverter is communicating normally but not generating. Causes: grid protection trip, DC isolation open, thermal cutout. More actionable than a missing-data event — the fault is in the inverter or its circuit, not the communication.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Check whether null periods are clustered at morning/evening transitions (MPPT startup threshold) or mid-day (protection trip).
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Préchac 2023, Sablirot Sud 2023

scada_data_quality_rules.name: First-Month Null Rate 15–25% Declining to <10% = Staggered Commissioning
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: At newly commissioned sites, a null-power rate of 15–25% in the first analysis month that drops to <10% in the second month indicates staggered inverter commissioning (inverters being progressively energised). Do not trigger false fault alarms during the first 4 weeks after commissioning.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Préchac 2023, Sablirot Sud 2023

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Seasonal SCADA Data Availability Collapse = Communication Infrastructure Fault
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: Seasonal SCADA data availability collapse (same calendar months across multiple years) affecting all inverters simultaneously is a SCADA/communication infrastructure fault. Check: (1) internet connection stability, (2) SCADA data logger buffer size — may overflow during high-production months, (3) seasonal temperature effects on communication hardware.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Mas d'En Ramis 2024

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Inverter PR > 110% = Asset Database Error
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: An inverter showing PR > 110% is a data quality error in the reference power figure, not genuine overperformance. Most common cause: incorrect or estimated installed capacity in asset database. Always verify PR denominators against the actual single-line diagram.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Mas d'En Ramis 2024 (PTR2 Inv06 showing PR 133–137%)

scada_data_quality_rules.name: PR > 3% Gap Between Independent Analyst and Client = Methodology Difference
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: When independent PR and client PR diverge by >3% consistently across months using the same input data, the discrepancy is methodological, not a plant fault. Investigate: (1) irradiation source differences (on-site pyranometer vs satellite), (2) irradiation weighting method (weighted by availability vs unweighted), (3) timestamp alignment between power and irradiance records.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Préchac 2023 (gap: 6.5 pp), Sablirot Sud 2023 (gap: 5.5 pp)

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Irradiance Sensor Drift — Systematic PR Bias
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: PV reference cells overestimate daily irradiance by >2% vs ISO 9060 Class A pyranometers due to cosine response and spectral mismatch — this makes plant PR appear lower than reality. Thermopile pyranometers drift +1 to +3%/year without heating/ventilation in humid climates, artificially degrading reported PR. Monthly comparison against PVGIS-SARAH3 satellite GHI is the primary QC tool.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Monthly ratio of on-site irradiation to PVGIS-SARAH3 reference. Deviation >3% on a monthly basis indicates sensor drift or failure.
actions: Annual cleaning and calibration of irradiance sensors (IEC 61724-1 Class A: weekly cleaning, 2-year calibration cycle)
For reference cell data: apply IEC 60904-7 spectral correction using measured air mass and precipitable water
Replace secondary pyranometers (Class C) with ISO 9060 Class A instruments for contractual PR reporting

scada_data_quality_rules.name: Stuck/Frozen Value Detection — Modbus Plants
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: Modbus RTU/TCP (used by most utility-scale string inverters including Sungrow) does not embed data quality flags. If a sensor stops updating its register, the last valid value is returned indefinitely — the SCADA receives data but it is stale. Requires application-layer stuck-value detection.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Apply frozen value test: if a channel returns the same value for K consecutive readings (e.g., K=5 at 5-min resolution = 25 min) during a period when the variable is expected to change (irradiance during variable cloud), flag as frozen. Exclude nighttime to avoid false positives on stable near-zero signals.

scada_data_quality_rules.name: MPPT Overload From DC Wiring Error — Automated Detection Rule
scada_data_quality_rules.rule: A single inverter with PR persistently 10–15% below the fleet average, without seasonal variation and without fault alarms, indicates a DC wiring configuration error (MPPT overload), not a module or inverter hardware fault. This condition can persist for years without O&M detection if automated alerting is absent.
scada_data_quality_rules.diagnostic: Automated alert: any inverter PR below 80% of fleet median for >30 consecutive days without active fault alarm should trigger a DC wiring audit.
sources: 8.2 France internal analysis — Mas d'En Ramis 2024 (PTR3 Inv13: PR 65–67% vs fleet 75–82%)
